profile.summary: A mid-level Product Designer owns end-to-end design work for defined product areas, from framing problems and flows to shipping polished UI with engineers and product managers. They turn research, data, and constraints into usable experiences, contribute to the design system, and handle day-to-day stakeholder collaboration with some guidance on bigger trade-offs and prioritisation.

known_challenges.text: Balancing speed to ship with enough discovery and iteration to avoid weak solutions.
Defending design decisions with evidence when product or engineering push for shortcuts.
Working through unclear requirements or shifting priorities without losing momentum.
Keeping craft quality high while working within technical, content, and accessibility constraints.
Juggling feedback from multiple stakeholders can create rework and drain energy.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Clarity of problem framing and priorities
recommended_question_themes.why: Mid-level designers do better work when the problem, success signals, and trade-offs are clear enough to act on.
Their impact depends heavily on day-to-day partnership with product managers, engineers, researchers, and content partners.
At this level they are expected to explain choices clearly, not just produce screens.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Space for craft, iteration, and accessibility
recommended_question_themes.why: Design quality often drops when delivery pressure leaves no room for refinement or inclusive design checks.
Frequent context switching and repeated critique cycles can quietly wear down motivation and focus.

terminology.meaning: The steps a user takes to complete a task.
A low-detail layout used to explore structure and flow.
A clickable model used to test or explain interactions.
How screens behave when people tap, type, or navigate.
Shared components, patterns, and rules for consistent product design.
Designing so people with different needs can use the product.
Early work to understand problems, users, and opportunities.
Structured feedback focused on improving the work.
Sharing final design details with engineers for build.
A short delivery cycle used by product and engineering teams.
Regular learning from users alongside delivery work.
Using AI tools to speed up concepts or interface drafts.
A less common scenario the design still needs to handle.
A measure used to judge whether the design worked.
A limit from tech, policy, time, or business needs.
A compromise between competing goals or limitations.
Watching people try tasks to find friction and confusion.

listen_for: Whether they talk about user problems and outcomes, not just screens and polish.
Whether they can explain trade-offs with product, engineering, and accessibility in mind.
Repeated mention of rework, late feedback, or unclear ownership.
How often they bring evidence from research, testing, or metrics into decisions.
Whether they avoid discussing stakeholder tension or seem stuck waiting for direction.
avoid: Treating them like a screen-maker instead of a partner in product decisions.
Pushing for polish without protecting time for discovery, testing, or accessibility.
Assuming stakeholder disagreement means they lack confidence or skill.
Giving vague feedback like "make it cleaner" without product context or constraints.
